title: 荣誉附件正文｜LOA Combine（第3部分）

To retain all knowledge from data streams, a straightforward approach is to store all the data that has arrived so far and then retrain the model with all the collected data. However, since data volume keeps on increasing and, on the contrary, the storage space is limited, it is impractical to maintain all the data for model training. To address such an issue, a strawman solution is using only the newly arrived stream data, not all the data collected so far, to update PLMs continuously. However, such a simple fine-tuning method has the Figure 1 Naive deployment of OCPLM2 A-2 RTDS S 22 14 05 1 following shortcomings. First, unlike a static corpus, the data stream is endless. If the model cannot finish learning the current batch of sentences before the next batch arrives, we need to either save the batches that have not yet been learned challenging the storage capacity (Tai et al. 2017) or drops future batches risking a lower quality of pretraining (Zhao, Viet Hung, and Weidlich 2020). Second, in contrast to the offline scenario where a model can be updated with all the historical data, we are only allowed to read data in one pass in OCPLM2. Consequently, model performance on past training data can degrade arbitrarily (often called catastrophic forgetting (Tiwari et al. 2022)), especially when the streaming data is not independent and identically distributed (i. e. , iid). New Approach: Stateful PLM Maintenance Framework Compared to prior works, we consider a challenging and realistic scenario where we continuously pre-trained the language model with massive amounts of streaming data generated in real-time. Our work is motivated by the following observations. First, the newly arrived data may involve redundancy or similar training samples (Kratzke 2020; Mirzasoleiman, Bilmes, and Leskovec 2020). Simply retrain models by using all incoming data points may not improve model accuracy. Second, trained models may already have acquired part of the knowledge from previous data points. Therefore, it is not necessary to use all new data, and repeated learning on areas of knowledge that are already sufficiently known makes little contribution to model performance. Third, compared to regularization-based and parameter isolation methods, rehearsal-based continual learning methods, maintaining and revising a small replay buffer, achieve promising results in addressing the challenges of catastrophic forgetting. Motivated by the observations, we propose a stateful PLM maintenance framework, where we maintain states (summary of data streams) to guide both data selection and updating of reply-buffers for rehearsal-based PLM maintenance. Figure 22 illustrates the overview of our proposed stateful PLM maintenance framework. Whenever a new streaming data batch arrives, it works in four stages: data selection, state update, buffer update, and model update. 1 data selection f(Bt ,St-1) Coreset Ct Batch Bt 2 updata State St St-1- > St 3 updata 4 Mt-1- > Mt 4 Replay buffer Model 𝜽 Mt Model update 𝜽t-1 - > 𝜽t Figure 2 Overview of Stateful PLM Maintenance Framework Stage 1: Data Selection.
